Skip to content

Accessibility Matrix for University of Southampton Brand Colour Palette

When we use our brand palette, we must ensure that the colours we choose for text and graphics have enough contrast to the background colour. Doing so means that the materials we create will be accessible to the largest audience. The table below lists all brand colour combinations. It shows which pairings have the required level of contrast for different uses. These uses include:

  • graphics and user interface elements
  • text and images of text.

View all examples of colour combinations suitable for: Graphical objects, text minimum (AA and AAA), and text enhanced (AAA).

Matrix controls
Filter results
Display results as...
Simulate colour vision deficiency
Filter columns
Choose focus colour

Colour Accessibility Matrix. Foreground colours are the columns, background colours are the rows. Where a foreground column and background row intersect the cell shows the Web Content Accessibility Guidline (WCAG) contrast complicance level of the two colours when used together.
Marine Blue University Blue White Midnight Blue Vibrant Blue Mid Blue Sky Blue Light Blue Digital Blue Black Warm Neutral 01 Warm Neutral 02 Warm Neutral 03 Warm Neutral 04 Warm Neutral 05 Warm Neutral 06 Cool Neutral 01 Cool Neutral 02 Cool Neutral 03 Cool Neutral 04 Cool Neutral 05 Marine 01 Marine 02 Marine 03 Marine 04 Marine 05 Digital Marine Forest 01 Forest 02 Forest 03 Forest 04 Forest 05 Digital Forest Horizon 01 Horizon 02 Horizon 03 Horizon 04 Horizon 05 Digital Horizon Amethyst 01 Amethyst 02 Amethyst 03 Amethyst 04 Amethyst 05 Digital Amethyst

Explaining the matrix.
Rating Explanation
F FAIL - 1241 of 2025 combinations (61%).
  • Colour combination should not be used for user interface (UI) components or graphical objects where no text is used, nor for the visual representation of text.
G Contrast ratio is at least 3:1 - 784 of 2025 combinations (39%).
  • Suitable for User Interface Components and Graphical Objects where no text is used (WCAG 2.1 Success Criterion 1.4.11 Non-text Contrast).
  • Suitable for large-scale text (at least 18 point if not bold and at least 14 point if bold) and images of large-scale text (WCAG 2.1 Success Criterion 1.4.3 Contrast (Minimum).
  • View all examples of brand colour combinations with a ratio of 3:1 and above.
AA Contrast ratio is at least 4.5:1 - 490 of 2025 combinations (24%).
  • Suitable for the visual representation of text and images of text to minimum level (WCAG 2.1 Success Criterion 1.4.3 Contrast (Minimum).
  • Suitable for large-scale text (at least 18 point if not bold and at least 14 point if bold) and images of large-scale text (WCAG 2.1 Success Criterion 1.4.6 Contrast (Enhanced)
  • Suitable for User Interface Components and Graphical Objects where no text is used (WCAG 2.1 Success Criterion 1.4.11 Non-text Contrast)
  • View all examples of brand colour combinations with a ratio of 4.5:1 and above.
AAA Contrast ratio is at least 7:1 - 278 of 2025 combinations (14%).
  • Suitable for the visual representation of text and images of text at enhanced level (WCAG 2.1 Success Criterion 1.4.6 Contrast (Enhanced).
  • Suitable for User Interface Components and Graphical Objects where no text is used (WCAG 2.1 Success Criterion 1.4.11 Non-text Contrast).
  • View all examples of brand colour combinations with a ratio of 7:1 and above.
NOTE Exclusions for visual representation of text are:
  • Incidental: Text or images of text that are part of an inactive user interface component, that are pure decoration, that are not visible to anyone, or that are part of a picture that contains significant other visual content, have no contrast requirement.
  • Logotypes: Text that is part of a logo or brand name has no contrast requirement.
More information about Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG)
View examples of all accessible colour combinations.

To help to choose accessible colour combinations follow these links to view examples of each possible accessible colour combination.

  • View all examples of brand colour combinations with a ratio of 3:1 and above (G rating).
  • View all examples of brand colour combinations with a ratio of 4.5:1 and above (AA rating).
  • View all examples of brand colour combinations with a ratio of 7:1 and above (AAA rating).
Download the data.
  • Colour contrast matrix using F, G, AA, and AAA ratings (Comma Separated File).
  • Colour contrast matrix showing contrast ratios (Comma Separated File).
  • Full dataset showing every colour combination, its contrast ratio and rating (Comma Separated File).
Want to learn more?
Credits

When you use colours from a defined palette, such as a corporate brand, you should make accessible choices. This colour accessibility matrix helps you do this. A custom bash script created by Matthew Deeprose, Senior Learning Designer at the University of Southampton, generated this web page.


Additional tools used are:

  • WebAIM Colour Contrast API.
  • JQ by Stephen Dolan and others.
  • csvtool by Jan Loeffler.
  • Tabulate.sh by Nathan S.R.
  • DaltonLens SVG filters.
  • System UIcons by Corey Ginnivan.
  • jQuery to help make some of the interactions easier to implement.

Whilst I have attempted to follow good practice and make this content as accessible as possible, there is always room for improvement. Send any feedback about the matrix and any suggestions for improvements to Matthew Deeprose by connecting on LinkedIn.

CC-BY-SA
Colour Accessibility Matrix by Matthew Deeprose is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution-ShareAlike 4.0 International License.
